message.content:
You are an independent fix-confirmation reviewer (reviewer B) for a production-hardening pass on the hc-assay repo at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. Be adversarial and skeptical — your job is to find any way the fix is wrong, incomplete, or its regression test fails to discriminate.

FINDING J-003 (MED):
CI editable install fetched the hatchling build backend LIVE and un-hashed from PyPI (an unscanned, non-reproducible build-time code-execution window) because builds used default build isolation. Fix: new requirements-build.lock (hashed hatchling/editables/transitive); CI installs it with --require-hashes then "pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e ." in all three lanes (core, integration, license-gate); a reproducibility-guard diff regenerates and compares requirements-build.lock. In .github/workflows/ci.yml + requirements-build.lock. Tests: test_ci_pins_build_backend_and_disables_live_build_isolation.

The complete code diff for all pass-7 fixes is at /tmp/pass7_code.diff (read it). The full files are under /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. You may run the test suite (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -m pytest -q <path>) — NOTE: use "python -m pytest", not bare "pytest", or the tests/ package won't import.

Judge ONLY finding J-003. Decide:
- appropriate: does the fix address the real root cause with a sound approach (not a band-aid, not the wrong layer)?
- complete: is there any remaining gap, AND does the regression test actually FAIL if the fix is reverted (i.e. does it discriminate)? If you can, reason about or test revert-discrimination.
Return CONFIRMED only if both are true. Return CONCERN if appropriate but with a real residual gap. Return REJECTED if the fix is wrong or the test is non-discriminating. List concrete concerns.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll review finding J-003. Let me start by reading the diff and relevant files.

83	diff --git a/scripts/capture_transcripts.py b/scripts/capture_transcripts.py
84	index 465013e..fb2ff89 100755
85	--- a/scripts/capture_transcripts.py
86	+++ b/scripts/capture_transcripts.py
87	@@ -73,7 +73,52 @@ def _username_pattern() -> re.Pattern[str] | None:
88	     return re.compile(re.escape(name))
89	 
90	 
91	+def _display_name_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
92	+    """Redactions for the operator's full DISPLAY NAME (#J-002).
93	+
94	+    The username/email rules leave the real name (e.g. a git ``user.name`` like "[REDACTED]")
95	+    in cleartext — distinct operator PII that appears in git author strings, ``pyproject``
96	+    ``authors=``, and prose, often right beside an already-redacted email. Derive candidate names
97	+    from ``git config user.name`` and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES`` env (comma-separated), redact each
98	+    (and, for a multi-word name, its whitespace-flexible form). Length-guarded to avoid
99	+    over-redacting common words.
100	+    """
101	+    names: list[str] = []
102	+    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES", "")
103	+    names.extend(n.strip() for n in extra.split(",") if n.strip())
104	+    # Derive the operator name from SEVERAL sources — any one may be empty in a given context
105	+    # (#J-002 follow-up): on this node local `git config user.name` is unset yet commits still
106	+    # carry an author via global config / env, so a single-source lookup scrubbed nothing. Cover
107	+    # the env author vars, git config, AND the actual last-commit author.
108	+    for env_name in ("GIT_AUTHOR_NAME", "GIT_COMMITTER_NAME"):
109	+        v = os.environ.get(env_name, "").strip()
110	+        if v:
111	+            names.append(v)
112	+    try:
113	+        import subprocess
114	+
115	+        for cmd in (["git", "config", "user.name"], ["git", "log", "-1", "--format=%an"]):
116	+            out = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
117	+            if out.returncode == 0 and out.stdout.strip():
118	+                names.append(out.stdout.strip())
119	+    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
120	+        pass
121	+    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
122	+    seen: set[str] = set()
123	+    for n in names:
124	+        if len(n) < 4 or n in seen:
125	+            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
126	+        seen.add(n)
127	+        # Flexible inter-token whitespace (e.g. "[REDACTED]"). NO leading \b: the name often
128	+        # appears glued to a JSON-escaped newline ("...---\\n[REDACTED]") where \b doesn't fire
129	+        # because the escape's 'n' abuts the name (#J-002 follow-up). A trailing \b still prevents
130	+        # matching inside a longer word (e.g. "Joneses"); the >=4-char guard bounds over-redaction.
131	+        pats.append(re.compile(r"\s+".join(re.escape(t) for t in n.split()) + r"\b"))
132	+    return pats
133	+
134	+
135	 _USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
136	+_DISPLAY_NAME_RES = _display_name_patterns()
137	 
138	 
139	 def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
140	@@ -88,6 +133,10 @@ def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
141	     # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
142	     if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
143	         text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
144	+    # Redact the operator's full display name too (#J-002) — distinct PII the username/email rules
145	+    # leave in cleartext (git author strings, pyproject authors=, prose).
146	+    for pat in _DISPLAY_NAME_RES:
147	+        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
148	     return text
149	 
150	 
151	@@ -104,13 +153,18 @@ def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
152	         # Scrub EVERY text file, not only .jsonl (#CV-O-1): the session subtree also holds .json
153	         # workflow/subagent transcripts that carry the same operator PII + credential-shaped
154	         # secrets — the old suffix==".jsonl" gate copied those RAW, bypassing redaction entirely.
155	-        # Decode strictly to classify: text → scrub; genuinely binary (rare here) → copy verbatim.
156	-        try:
157	-            text = raw.decode("utf-8")
158	-        except UnicodeDecodeError:
159	-            data = raw  # binary blob — nothing to scrub
160	+        #
161	+        # FAIL CLOSED on redaction (#J-001): classify binary by an actual binary SIGNAL (a NUL
162	+        # byte), NOT by UTF-8 validity. A transcript routinely embeds arbitrary subprocess stdout
163	+        # (latin-1 dumps, truncated multibyte) inside JSONL strings; gating on strict decode meant
164	+        # a single stray byte routed the WHOLE file to the verbatim branch and re-leaked it (the
165	+        # CV-O-1 fix had turned an always-redact path into a fail-open one). Decode with
166	+        # errors="replace" so redaction ALWAYS runs on text; only a genuinely binary blob (NUL
167	+        # present) is copied verbatim.
168	+        if b"\x00" in raw:
169	+            data = raw  # genuinely binary — nothing to scrub
170	         else:
171	-            data = _scrub(text).encode("utf-8")
172	+            data = _scrub(raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")).encode("utf-8")
173	         if dstf.exists() and dstf.read_bytes() == data:
174	             pass  # unchanged — still report it in the manifest
175	         else:

182	         if exp is not None:
183	             return str(exp.experiment_id)
184	-        return str(client.create_experiment(self._experiment))
185	+        # check-then-create is a TOCTOU (#J-004): two concurrent first-time start_run calls (the
186	+        # 8-thread reasoning pool, or multiple study processes on one shared local store) can both
187	+        # see None and both create_experiment; MLflow rejects the loser with RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS
188	+        # and the exception would propagate out of start_run, leaving run_id=None and silently
189	+        # dropping ALL metrics + the provenance artifact for the whole study. On a create race,
190	+        # re-fetch the now-existing experiment instead of failing.
191	+        try:
192	+            return str(client.create_experiment(self._experiment))
193	+        except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - normalize the concurrent-create race to a re-fetch

213	+    assert not isinstance(excinfo.value, (RateLimitError, PermanentReasoningError)), (

249	diff --git a/tests/test_observability.py b/tests/test_observability.py

253	@@ -123,6 +123,32 @@ def test_bootstrap_logs_warning_on_setup_failure(monkeypatch, caplog):
254	     assert any("setup failed" in r.message for r in caplog.records)
255

257	+def test_experiment_id_survives_concurrent_create_race(monkeypatch, tmp_path):
258	+    # #J-004: check-then-create is a TOCTOU — on the first-time race, create_experiment raises
259	+    # RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS for the loser. _experiment_id must re-fetch the now-existing
260	+    # experiment instead of propagating (which would leave run_id=None and drop all metrics).
261	+    monkeypatch.setenv("ASSAY_TRACKING_URI", f"sqlite:///{tmp_path / 'x.db'}")
262	+
263	+    class _Exp:
264	+        experiment_id = "exp-7"
265	+
266	+    class RacingClient:
267	+        def __init__(self):
268	+            self.calls = 0
269	+
270	+        def get_experiment_by_name(self, name):
271	+            # first call (the check) sees nothing; after the failed create, the winner's exists
272	+            self.calls += 1
273	+            return None if self.calls == 1 else _Exp()
274	+
275	+        def create_experiment(self, name):
276	+            raise RuntimeError("RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS: another writer created it")
277	+
278	+    t = MlflowExperimentTracker()
279	+    client = RacingClient()
280	+    assert t._experiment_id(client) == "exp-7"  # re-fetched, not raised
281	+
282	+
283	 def test_start_run_terminates_run_when_param_logging_fails(monkeypatch, tmp_path):
284	     # #F-023: if create_run succeeds but log_param fails, the run must be terminated FAILED, not
285	     # left orphaned in RUNNING state. Mock the MLflow client to exercise the failure path without
286	diff --git a/tests/test_scripts.py b/tests/test_scripts.py
287	index 510b00f..b66eceb 100644
288	--- a/tests/test_scripts.py
289	+++ b/tests/test_scripts.py
290	@@ -123,6 +123,39 @@ def test_copy_scrubbed_copies_binary_verbatim(tmp_path):
291	     assert dst.read_bytes() == b"\xff\xfe\x00\x01binary"
292	 
293	 
294	+def test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes():
295	+    # #J-001: a stray non-UTF-8 byte must NOT disable redaction for the whole file. The scrubber
296	+    # decodes errors='replace' and classifies binary by a NUL byte, so a secret next to an invalid
297	+    # byte is still scrubbed (the pass-6 strict-decode fix had made this fail OPEN).
298	+    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_failclosed")
299	+    raw = b'{"out":"[REDACTED] caf\xe9 /Users/[REDACTED]/x"}'  # invalid \xe9
300	+    text = raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")
301	+    out = cap._scrub(text)
302	+    assert "[REDACTED]" not in out  # secret scrubbed despite the bad byte
303	+    assert "somebody" not in out
304	+
305	+
306	+def test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8(tmp_path):
307	+    # #J-001 end-to-end: a .jsonl with an invalid byte is still scrubbed on copy, not copied raw.
308	+    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_fc2")
309	+    src = tmp_path / "t.jsonl"
310	+    src.write_bytes(b'{"k":"[REDACTED] \x80 end"}')  # invalid \x80
311	+    dst = tmp_path / "out" / "t.jsonl"
312	+    cap._copy_scrubbed(src, dst)
313	+    out = dst.read_bytes()
314	+    assert b"abcdefghijklmnopqrstuv" not in out and b"[REDACTED]" in out
315	+
316	+
317	+def test_scrub_redacts_operator_display_name(monkeypatch):
318	+    # #J-002: the operator's full display name (from ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES / git user.name) is redacted,
319	+    # not just the email — distinct PII that leaked beside redacted emails.
320	+    monkeypatch.setenv("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES", "Ada Lovelace")
321	+    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_name")
322	+    out = cap._scrub("Author: Ada Lovelace <[REDACTED]>; also Ada   Lovelace in prose")
323	+    assert "Ada Lovelace" not in out and "Ada   Lovelace" not in out  # flexible whitespace too
324	+    assert "[REDACTED]" not in out  # email still scrubbed
325	+

338	+def test_ci_pins_build_backend_and_disables_live_build_isolation():
339	+    # #J-003: the editable install must use the hashed build backend (requirements-build.lock) with
340	+    # --no-build-isolation, so hatchling isn't fetched live/un-hashed from PyPI (an unscanned,
341	+    # non-reproducible build-time code-exec window).

353	+    # (--with-system pulls system tooling that can spuriously fail or mask the gate).

356	+        "license gate still --with-system"

362	     # tag can be force-pushed to malicious code). A version tag is allowed only as a comment.

1	name: ci
2	
3	on:
4	  push:
5	    branches: [main]
6	  pull_request:
7	
8	# Least privilege (#H-008): the default GITHUB_TOKEN can be repo-write; this workflow only reads
9	# the code and uploads artifacts, so pin the token to read-only at the top level. Any job needing
10	# more must opt in explicitly with its own `permissions:`.
11	permissions:
12	  contents: read
13	
14	jobs:
15	  # Dependency-free core: the engine must lint, type-check, and test with NO extras installed
16	  # (optional backends are import-guarded; integration tests skip). This guards ADR-0006.
17	  core:
18	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
19	    strategy:
20	      matrix:
21	        python-version: ["3.11", "3.12", "3.13", "3.14"]
22	    steps:
23	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
24	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
25	        with:
26	          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
27	      - name: Install (no extras, hash-pinned)
28	        run: |
29	          # Install the dev toolchain from the hashed, audited core lockfile so tests run against
30	          # EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned and the SBOM describes — not a live PyPI
31	          # resolution where a malicious patch release could execute under test (#F-004). The
32	          # core lock carries only the `dev` extra (no backends), preserving the ADR-0006 guard.
33	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
34	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-core.lock
35	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
36	          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
37	      - name: Lint (ruff)
38	        run: ruff check src tests
39	      - name: Format check (ruff)
40	        run: ruff format --check src tests
41	      - name: Type-check (mypy)
42	        run: mypy
43	      - name: Test (pytest)
44	        run: pytest -q
45	
46	  # Extras lane: install the real backends and run the integration suite so the backend code
47	  # paths actually execute, and mypy type-checks against the REAL (not ignored) library types.
48	  # Service-dependent tests (ollama/qdrant/postgres) skip when the service is absent in CI; the
49	  # extra-only tests (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, numpy primitives, methodology
50	  # e2e) run for real.
51	  integration:
52	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
53	    services:
54	      postgres:
55	        image: postgres:16
56	        env:
57	          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
58	          POSTGRES_DB: assay
59	        ports: ["5432:5432"]
60	        options: >-
61	          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U postgres"
62	          --health-interval 5s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 10
63	      qdrant:
64	        image: qdrant/qdrant:v1.12.4
65	        ports: ["6333:6333"]
66	    env:
67	      # loopback host keeps the data-sovereignty guard satisfied; creds are redacted on error
68	      ASSAY_TEST_POSTGRES_URL: postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/assay
69	    steps:
70	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
71	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
72	        with:
73	          python-version: "3.12"
74	      - name: Install (all extras, hash-pinned)
75	        run: |
76	          # Install from the hashed all-extras lockfile so the integration suite executes against
77	          # exactly the audited, pinned versions — closing the window where a compromised patch
78	          # release within a pyproject range could run during the real-backend tests (#F-004).
79	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
80	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
81	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
82	          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
83	      - name: Type-check against real backend types (mypy)
84	        run: mypy
85	      - name: Verify service containers are reachable (no silent skip)
86	        # #H-009: the live Postgres/Qdrant tests SKIP when the service is unreachable, so a
87	        # misconfigured/never-started container would let the integration job report GREEN without
88	        # actually exercising the backends. Fail loudly here if either service isn't up, so the
89	        # gate cannot pass vacuously.
90	        run: |
91	          set -euo pipefail
92	          for i in $(seq 1 30); do
93	            pg_isready -h localhost -p 5432 -U postgres && break || sleep 2
94	            [ "$i" = "30" ] && { echo "::error::Postgres service never became ready"; exit 1; }
95	          done
96	          for i in $(seq 1 30); do
97	            curl -fsS http://localhost:6333/readyz && break || sleep 2
98	            [ "$i" = "30" ] && { echo "::error::Qdrant service never became ready"; exit 1; }
99	          done
100	          echo "postgres + qdrant reachable"
101	      # Real backends now run: langgraph graph + interrupt/resume, mlflow lifecycle, OTel,
102	      # numpy primitives, the T3 drain/classification (synthetic SDK, no metered call), AND —
103	      # via the service containers — the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store.
104	      # Ollama and the metered frontier tier are not in CI and skip honestly.
105	      - name: Integration + unit tests with coverage
106	        run: pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing
107	
108	  # Supply-chain: fail on known-vulnerable dependencies (a security/privacy engine must not ship
109	  # with advisories). The scan runs against the committed, fully-pinned, hashed lockfile
110	  # (requirements.lock) so the audit is reproducible across runs and matches what installs
111	  # (#108, #130, #131, #132, #145, #146). An allowlist file (.pip-audit-ignore, one advisory id
112	  # per line, '#' comments and blank lines allowed) covers accepted/unfixable ones.
113	  audit:
114	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
115	    steps:
116	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
117	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
118	        with:
119	          python-version: "3.12"
120	      - name: Install pip-audit
121	        # Pin the audit tooling to exact versions (#G-022): an unpinned pip-audit can change
122	        # advisory results / exit behavior between releases, making the supply-chain gate
123	        # non-reproducible (a green commit could fail tomorrow on a tooling bump) — the analogue
124	        # of the uv pin (#F-031).
125	        run: python -m pip install --upgrade pip && python -m pip install pip-audit==2.10.1
126	      - name: Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject (reproducibility guard)
127	        run: |
128	          # Pin uv to the exact version the lockfiles were generated with (#F-031): an unpinned
129	          # `uv` upgrade can change output format/ordering/annotations and produce spurious diffs
130	          # that fail this gate on every PR even when the lockfiles are current.
131	          python -m pip install uv==0.11.21
132	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements.regen.lock
133	          # ignore the auto-generated header (timestamp/command line) when diffing
134	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements.regen.lock) \
135	            || { echo "::error::requirements.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements.lock"; exit 1; }
136	          # the core lockfile (dev extra only) backs the hash-pinned core lane (#F-004) and must
137	          # stay in sync too.
138	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock
139	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements-core.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock) \
140	            || { echo "::error::requirements-core.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-core.lock"; exit 1; }
141	          # the build-backend lockfile (hatchling etc.) backs the hash-pinned --no-build-isolation
142	          # editable install (#J-003) and must stay in sync.
143	          printf 'hatchling\neditables\n' > /tmp/build-reqs.in
144	          uv pip compile /tmp/build-reqs.in --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock
145	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements-build.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock) \
146	            || { echo "::error::requirements-build.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile <(printf 'hatchling\\neditables\\n') --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-build.lock"; exit 1; }
147	      - name: pip-audit (reproducible known-vulnerability scan)
148	        run: |
149	          set -euo pipefail
150	          # --strict: any dependency that cannot be collected/audited FAILS the gate (no silent
151	          # skips). --require-hashes: every pin must carry a hash. Build --ignore-vuln args as an
152	          # array so allowlist entries can't word-split or glob-expand, and read the file
153	          # newline-agnostically so the last line is honored even without a trailing newline.
154	          IGNORES=()
155	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
156	            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
157	              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
158	              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
159	            done < .pip-audit-ignore
160	          fi
161	          pip-audit --strict --require-hashes --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock "${IGNORES[@]}"
162	      - name: SBOM (CycloneDX) from the lockfile
163	        run: |
164	          set -euo pipefail
165	          # Emit a machine-readable software bill of materials for the pinned dependency set so
166	          # downstream license/transitive monitoring has a stable artifact to track (#147). No
167	          # `|| true` mask (#F-012): a pip-audit failure must fail the step, and the SBOM must be
168	          # non-empty, so a broken/absent SBOM cannot pass CI green.
169	          # Apply the SAME allowlist as the gate (#G-015): pip-audit exits non-zero on ANY finding
170	          # regardless of output format, so without the allowlist an ACCEPTED advisory would fail
171	          # SBOM generation here even though the gate step passed — coupling SBOM emission to vuln
172	          # status and making the allowlist ineffective. The gate step already ran first, so a
173	          # NON-allowlisted advisory has failed the job before we reach here.
174	          IGNORES=()
175	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
176	            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
177	              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
178	              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
179	            done < .pip-audit-ignore
180	          fi
181	          pip-audit --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json "${IGNORES[@]}"
182	          test -s sbom.cyclonedx.json
183	          echo "SBOM emitted ($(wc -c < sbom.cyclonedx.json) bytes)"
184	      - name: Upload SBOM artifact
185	        if: always()
186	        uses: actions/upload-artifact@ea165f8d65b6e75b540449e92b4886f43607fa02 # v4
187	        with:
188	          name: sbom-cyclonedx
189	          path: sbom.cyclonedx.json
190	          if-no-files-found: error
191	      - name: License gate (deny strong-copyleft in the pinned dependency set)
192	        run: |
193	          set -euo pipefail
194	          # Fail if any dependency carries a strong-copyleft license incompatible with the
195	          # Apache-2.0 distribution (#147). Scan the SAME hash-pinned set pip-audit audited, not a
196	          # live `pip install -e .[all]` resolution that could pull different versions than the
197	          # lockfile pins (#F-013). Advisory monitoring of the rest is handled by Dependabot.
198	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
199	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
200	          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
201	          python -m pip install pip-licenses==5.5.5  # pinned for reproducible license output (#G-022)
202	          pip-licenses --format=json > licenses.json  # scan the dep set, not system tooling (#J-006)
203	          python scripts/license_gate.py licenses.json
204	      - name: Upload license report
205	        if: always()
206	        uses: actions/upload-artifact@ea165f8d65b6e75b540449e92b4886f43607fa02 # v4
207	        with:
208	          name: license-report
209	          path: licenses.json
210	          if-no-files-found: warn
211	
212	  # Single required status check (#F-014): branch protection should require ONLY `all-checks`.
213	  # It fans in every other job, so adding a new job automatically gates merges without anyone
214	  # remembering to update the branch-protection rule — and `audit`/`integration` can never be
215	  # silently dropped from the merge gate by requiring just `core`.
216	  all-checks:
217	    if: always()
218	    needs: [core, integration, audit]
219	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
220	    steps:
221	      - name: Verify all required jobs succeeded
222	        run: |
223	          results="${{ needs.core.result }} ${{ needs.integration.result }} ${{ needs.audit.result }}"
224	          echo "job results: $results"
225	          for r in $results; do
226	            if [ "$r" != "success" ]; then
227	              echo "::error::a required job did not succeed ($r)"
228	              exit 1
229	            fi
230	          done
231	          echo "all required jobs succeeded"
232

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && head -40 tests/test_supply_chain.py && echo "---ROOT/CI helpers---" && grep -n "_ci_text\|_ROOT" tests/test_supply_chain.py | head

content.content:
"""Supply-chain / CI audit-gate regression guards (pass-2 #130, #131, #132, #145, #146, #147).

These guard configuration that lives in .github/workflows/ci.yml and the committed lockfile.
The CI YAML cannot be unit-imported, so the guards are twofold:
  1. config-level: assert the hardened invocation is present in ci.yml (a revert to the buggy
     form fails the test);
  2. behavioral: run the exact allowlist-parsing bash loop against fixtures to prove it is
     newline-agnostic and comment/blank tolerant (the original `while read -r id` form drops a
     final line that lacks a trailing newline).
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import shutil
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path

import pytest

_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
_CI = _ROOT / ".github" / "workflows" / "ci.yml"
_LOCK = _ROOT / "requirements.lock"

# The hardened allowlist loop, lifted verbatim from ci.yml's audit job. Kept here so the
# behavioral test exercises the SAME logic the workflow runs.
_PARSE_LOOP = r"""
set -euo pipefail
IGNORES=()
if [ -f "$1" ]; then
  while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
    case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
    IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
  done < "$1"
fi
printf '%s\n' "${IGNORES[@]:-<none>}"
"""

# The ORIGINAL buggy loop, for the revert-discrimination assertion in the behavioral test.
_PARSE_LOOP_BUGGY = r"""
IGNORES=""
---ROOT/CI helpers---
20:_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
21:_CI = _ROOT / ".github" / "workflows" / "ci.yml"
22:_LOCK = _ROOT / "requirements.lock"
48:def _ci_text() -> str:
55:    text = _ci_text()
63:    text = _ci_text()
87:    text = _ci_text()
94:    text = _ci_text()
115:    pp = tomllib.loads((_ROOT / "pyproject.toml").read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
130:    db = _ROOT / ".github" / "dependabot.yml"

content.text: The pyproject build-system requires `hatchling` (and hatchling pulls in `editables` for editable installs). The lock covers hatchling + editables + transitives. Now let me run the regression test and verify revert-discrimination.
Now let me test revert-discrimination by simulating the reverted CI form against the test logic.

content.content:

50	
51	
52	def test_pip_audit_runs_strict_and_require_hashes():
53	    # #130: without --strict an unauditable dependency is silently skipped while CI stays green.
54	    # --require-hashes ties the scan to the pinned, hashed lockfile.
55	    text = _ci_text()

content.text:
Now the key revert-discrimination question. The test asserts:
1. `"--no-build-isolation --no-deps -e ." in text`
2. `"requirements-build.lock" in text`
3. lockfile exists with `hatchling==` and `--hash=sha256:`
4. `"pip install --no-deps -e .\n" not in text` (old form gone)

Let me verify the old reverted form would actually trip assertion #1 and #4. The original line was `pip install --no-deps -e .`. Let me check there are no other `--no-deps -e .` occurrences that would mask the discrimination.

content.content:
35:          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
36:          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
81:          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
82:          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
141:          # the build-backend lockfile (hatchling etc.) backs the hash-pinned --no-build-isolation
144:          uv pip compile /tmp/build-reqs.in --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock
145:          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements-build.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock) \
146:            || { echo "::error::requirements-build.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile <(printf 'hatchling\\neditables\\n') --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-build.lock"; exit 1; }
199:          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)

content.text:
All three lanes patched. Now let me reason about revert-discrimination carefully. If the fix is reverted to `pip install --no-deps -e .`:
- Assertion #1 (`--no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .`) would FAIL since the string no longer present. Good, it discriminates.

Let me verify the regenerated lockfile actually matches what's committed (the reproducibility-guard the fix introduces). This is important — if the committed lock doesn't match `uv pip compile`, the CI guard would fail on every run.

/opt/homebrew/bin/uv
uv 0.11.21 (Homebrew 2026-06-11 aarch64-apple-darwin)
    --hash=sha256:ab4c4ec93cc4a4e7815fa759906e05e6bb3f2fbd92ea0f897288c6a43efd15b3 \
    --hash=sha256:c5132b4b61a829d11cfbd2d72e97f20a45ed6edb95e45c5efdeb5e00836b2745
    # via hatchling
=== DIFF (non-comment) ===
MATCH

content.text:
The committed lock matches the regen exactly with the pinned uv 0.11.21, so the reproducibility guard will pass. Now, one concern: the build lock pins `hatchling==1.30.1` but pyproject says `requires = ["hatchling"]` (unbounded). Could a `--no-build-isolation` install with hatchling 1.30.1 satisfy an unbounded requirement? Yes — any version satisfies unbounded. Good.

Let me check a subtle gap: does `--no-build-isolation` actually use the pre-installed hatchling? With `--no-deps`, pip won't install build deps, and with `--no-build-isolation` it uses the current env's hatchling. Both flags are present. Let me also verify there's no `pip install build` or wheel needed. Let me actually attempt the install locally to confirm it works (the real behavioral proof).

content.text: That failure is an artifact of my test venv using Python 3.9 (system python3), where hatchling 1.30.1 requires >=3.10. CI uses Python 3.11-3.14. Let me redo with the project's venv python.

content.text:
The hashed build lock installs cleanly under the real CI python versions, and the `--no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .` editable install succeeds using the pre-installed, hashed hatchling. The fix works end to end.

Let me do a final revert-discrimination check by confirming the test assertions truly catch a revert. Let me also confirm the full test file passes (J-006 assertion shares this finding's diff but is separate).

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -m pytest -q tests/test_supply_chain.py 2>&1 | tail -5; echo "=== simulate revert: does assertion catch it? ==="; python3 -c "
text = open('.github/workflows/ci.yml').read()
# simulate revert
reverted = text.replace('pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)\n          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .', 'pip install --no-deps -e .')
print('after revert, --no-build-isolation present:', '--no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .' in reverted)
print('after revert, old form present:', 'pip install --no-deps -e .\n' in reverted)
print('requirements-build.lock still referenced:', 'requirements-build.lock' in reverted)
"
input.description: Run full supply-chain tests and simulate revert

content.text:
Revert-discrimination confirmed: on a revert, `--no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .` disappears → assertion #1 fails. The test discriminates.

One residual nuance worth noting: after a revert, the old-form assertion `"pip install --no-deps -e .\n" not in text` would ALSO fail (old form present True). And assertion #1 fails too. So two independent assertions catch the revert. Strong discrimination.

Let me consider any remaining gap on appropriateness. The fix only locks `hatchling` and `editables` explicitly in the `.in` file; pyproject only requires `hatchling`. `editables` is hatchling's editable-install runtime dep, correctly included. The transitive set (packaging, pathspec, pluggy, trove-classifiers) is captured with hashes. The reproducibility guard regenerates and diffs. One minor consideration: the build lock is generated WITHOUT `--python-version` constraint, but it resolved to versions valid across 3.11-3.14 (verified install on 3.14). Good.

A subtle theoretical concern: `--no-build-isolation` means pip uses whatever is in the env. Since the build lock is installed immediately before with `--require-hashes`, and `--no-deps` on the editable prevents pulling anything else, the build backend is exactly the hashed set. Sound.

input.rationale:
The fix addresses the real root cause: default build isolation made `pip install -e .` fetch hatchling LIVE and un-hashed from PyPI at build time, an unscanned code-execution window. The fix pins the build backend (hatchling 1.30.1 + editables + transitives, all sha256-hashed) in a new requirements-build.lock, installs it with --require-hashes, then runs `pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .` so the editable build uses ONLY that hashed, pre-installed backend — in all three lanes (core, integration, license-gate). A reproducibility guard in the audit job regenerates the build lock with the pinned uv 0.11.21 and diffs it. This is the correct layer (build-time supply chain), not a band-aid.

I verified end to end: (1) the committed requirements-build.lock matches `uv pip compile` output exactly under the pinned uv 0.11.21 (guard will pass, not spuriously fail); (2) installing the hashed build lock then `pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .` succeeds under the real CI python (3.14.5), proving the pre-installed hashed hatchling actually services the editable build (my earlier 3.9 failure was just my system-python artifact; CI uses 3.11-3.14 which all satisfy hatchling's >=3.10). (3) The regression test passes, and simulating a revert to `pip install --no-deps -e .` makes assertion `"--no-build-isolation --no-deps -e ." in text` go False AND the `"pip install --no-deps -e .\n" not in text` assertion go False — two independent assertions discriminate the revert. editables is hatchling's editable-runtime dep and is correctly included; pyproject's unbounded `requires=["hatchling"]` is satisfied by the pinned version. Both appropriate and complete.

content.text: CONFIRMED. The J-003 fix is appropriate and complete, with a discriminating regression test. I verified the committed build lock reproduces exactly under the pinned uv, the hashed `--no-build-isolation` editable install works end to end on the real CI python version, and a simulated revert trips the regression assertions.
